title: The Scandinavian & Meeting 1.e4
subtitle: 1.e4 d5 — strike the centre at once, keep the queen safe, and punish 1.e4's cheap traps

concepts.name: The Scandinavian strike
concepts.short: 1...d5 hits e4 the instant it appears, refusing White a free big centre.

concepts.name: The queen tempo
concepts.short: Recapturing 2...Qxd5 brings the queen out early; Nc3 hits her, so she must retreat to a SAFE active square.

concepts.name: Queen safety
concepts.short: An early queen is a target. Park her where no minor piece can chase her for free, and cover the b5 square with ...c6.

concepts.name: The Scandinavian set-up
concepts.short: After the queen is safe: ...Nf6, ...c6, ...Bf5 (out before ...e6), ...e6, ...Be7/...g6, castle. A solid, hard-to-break house.

concepts.name: Punish the passive reply
concepts.short: If White declines (2.Nc3) or pushes (2.e5), Black equalises instantly by grabbing the pawn or hitting the chain with ...c5.

concepts.name: Meeting 1.e4's traps
concepts.short: Know the cheap shots: the Wayward Queen (2.Qh5) eyes f7 — answer with ...g6 and ...Nf6; never weaken f7 with ...f6 (Damiano).

lessons.title: The Strike: 1...d5 and the Queen Tempo
lessons.summary: Why 1...d5 is a fighting answer to 1.e4, and how to handle the queen after 2...Qxd5 3.Nc3.

intro.text: Before we open a new defence, sharpen the eye. In every position one move wins material outright. The Scandinavian leads to open, piece-active play — these are the patterns you'll be hunting.
I am Viktor. Today, Black. When White plays 1.e4 he stakes a claim in the centre. Most defences let that pawn sit. The Scandinavian does not — we answer 1...d5 and hit e4 on move one.

coach.text: White has just played 1.e4. He wants 2.d4 next and a wall of pawns on e4 and d4. We refuse to allow it.
1...d5! Straight at e4. White cannot defend the pawn well, so he almost always takes: 2.exd5. Good — we have traded his proud centre pawn for a flank pawn, and the centre is half-open already.

After 2.exd5 we recapture with the queen: 2...Qxd5. Yes — the queen comes out early. That breaks a rule you learned, so you must respect WHY: she is exposed, and White will gain a tempo by attacking her. We must be ready to retreat her to safety.

3.Nc3! There it is — the knight develops AND attacks the queen. White gains a tempo. This is the price of the Scandinavian, and it is small: he spends a move chasing, we spend a move retreating, and the position is roughly balanced. The only mistake would be to retreat her to a square where she is chased AGAIN.

intro.text: The knight on c3 attacks your queen on d5. Move her to a safe, active square. Two squares are the classical homes; a third quiet retreat is also fine.

coach.text: Black to move. The queen is attacked. Where does she belong? Find a square where she is active and not hanging.

successLine.text: Good. 3...Qa5 (pinning thoughts on c3) and 3...Qd6 are the two main homes; 3...Qd8 is solid but passive. What you must NEVER do is 3...Qe5+? or 3...Qe6+? — White just blocks with a developing move and chases the queen for free.

retryLine.text: Not there. A check like ...Qe5+ feels active, but White answers Be2 or Nge2 — developing WITH tempo — and your queen is shoved around again. Retreat to a5, d6, or d8 instead.

intro.text: The Scandinavian opens lines. Finish with tactics from open middlegames — the kind your active pieces will create.

lessons.title: The Main Line: 3...Qa5
lessons.summary: The classical 3...Qa5 and Black's full development scheme.

intro.text: 3...Qa5 is the old main line. The queen eyes the knight on c3 and the a5–e1 diagonal, and she sits far from White's pieces — hard to hit again.

coach.text: 3...Qa5. Notice the queen now pins NOTHING yet, but she pressures c3 and is safe on the rim. White's natural plan is 4.d4 and 5.Nf3, building his centre. We answer with quick, harmonious development.

White has played 4.d4 and 5.Nf3. Our move order: 5...c6! first. This little pawn does two big jobs — it gives the queen a retreat square on c7 and, crucially, it covers b5 so the knight can never jump in with Nb5 to harass her. Queen safety again.

After 6.Bc4 we play 6...Bf5! The light-squared bishop comes OUT of the pawn chain before we play ...e6 and lock it in. This is the secret to the Scandinavian: never bury that bishop behind ...e6. He sits on f5 eyeing White's kingside.

The finished house: ...e6, ...Be7, ...O-O all played. Bishop on f5 is active, pawns on c6/e6 form a wall, the queen on a5 is safe, the king is castled. White stands a shade better — he has the extra centre tempo — but Black is rock-solid and ready to play ...Nbd7 and fight with ...c5 or ...Rad8. This is a full, sound game.

intro.text: Watch it flow from move one. I'll name the purpose of each Black move. Memorise the IDEAS, not just the squares.

coach.text: 1.e4 — White claims the centre.
1...d5! We strike e4 at once.
2.exd5 — he grabs the pawn.
2...Qxd5 — recapture. The queen is out, but we know the plan.
3.Nc3 — develop with tempo, hitting the queen.
3...Qa5 — retreat to a safe, active square.
4.d4 — White builds his centre.
4...Nf6 — develop, eye e4 and d5.
5.Nf3 — White's last minor piece toward the centre.
5...c6! — guard b5, give the queen the c7 escape.
6.Bc4 — White's bishop aims at f7.
6...Bf5! — bishop OUT before ...e6.
7.Bd2 — developing, eyeing the queen on a5.
7...e6 — now lock the chain; the bishop is already outside it on f5.
8.O-O — White tucks his king away.
8...Be7 — modest but solid, preparing to castle.
9.Re1 — White centralises a rook.
9...O-O — the house is complete: king safe, all minor pieces out, the f5-bishop active. White has a small space edge, Black is rock-solid with ...Nbd7 and a later ...c5 or ...Rad8 to come.

intro.text: Your f5-bishop and active pieces create threats. Train the eye for trapped and undefended minor pieces.

lessons.title: The Modern Line: 3...Qd6
lessons.summary: 3...Qd6 — the flexible modern queen retreat, with the same plan.

intro.text: Most strong players today prefer 3...Qd6. The queen guards more squares from d6, supports a future ...e5, and is even harder to attack than on a5.

coach.text: 3...Qd6. The queen sits in the centre but on a square no minor piece can reach with tempo. She defends the kingside and keeps the option of ...e5 to free the position. Same idea, slightly safer address.

The set-up mirrors the main line: 4.d4 Nf6 5.Nf3 c6. Again ...c6 first — and from d6 the queen would have to dodge a Nb5 jump, so covering b5 with ...c6 matters even more here. Queen safety is the through-line of this whole defence.

Then 6...Bf5, ...e6, ...Be7, ...Nbd7, ...O-O. Identical scheme to the 3...Qa5 line — that is the beauty: learn ONE plan and it serves both queen retreats. White keeps a small space edge; Black is solid and ready to counter with ...c5 or ...e5 later.

intro.text: Prove you've absorbed the move order. White has played 4.d4 and 5.Nf3 against your 3...Qd6. Play the key prophylactic pawn move.

coach.text: Black to move. Before developing the bishop, play the little pawn move that guards b5 and gives the queen an escape.

successLine.text: 5...c6! Exactly. Now Nb5 is impossible, the queen has c7 if needed, and you're ready for ...Bf5 and ...e6. The same house goes up no matter which retreat you chose.

retryLine.text: Not yet. Develop later — first prevent White's tricks. Which pawn move stops Nb5 AND gives the queen the c7 escape square? It's ...c6.

intro.text: From these solid structures the win comes from a well-timed break. Train tactics in the 1250–1340 zone.

lessons.title: Punishing White's Bad Replies
lessons.summary: When White declines (2.Nc3) or pushes (2.e5), Black equalises at once.

intro.text: Not every White player captures on d5. The two off-beat tries — declining with 2.Nc3 and pushing with 2.e5 — are not dangerous if you know the reply.

coach.text: 2.Nc3?! — White defends e4 instead of taking. But now we have a free choice. The clean reply is 2...dxe4 3.Nxe4, after which we develop with ...Nf6, ...Bf5 and have an easy, equal game — White has nothing.

2.e5?! — White pushes past instead of taking. This is just a worse French Defence for him: his pawn on e5 can be attacked at the base. We hit it with 2...c5! and follow with ...Nc6, ...Bf5, ...e6. Black is at least equal — the e5-pawn is a long-term weakness.

intro.text: White has just played 2.Nc3?!, declining the pawn. Take the most direct route to equality.

coach.text: Black to move. White defended e4 with 2.Nc3. What is the simplest way to a comfortable game?

successLine.text: 2...dxe4 — simplest and best. After 3.Nxe4 you've removed White's only centre pawn and develop freely with ...Nf6 and ...Bf5. (Pushing 2...d4 to gain space is also perfectly good.) White's 2.Nc3 gave you an easy game.

retryLine.text: There's a cleaner path. White's knight DEFENDS e4 but you can still resolve the centre. Capture the pawn — 2...dxe4 — or shove yours forward with ...d4.

intro.text: Against the 2.e5 push you'll undermine a pawn chain. Train tactics that exploit overworked and undefended pawns and pieces.

lessons.title: Meeting 1.e4's Cheap Traps
lessons.summary: The Wayward Queen (2.Qh5), the f7 mate, and the f6 weakness (Damiano) — and the queen-greed trap to avoid.

intro.text: Below master level you will meet the early queen sortie 2.Qh5 (here against 1...e5, but the idea is universal): White lunges at f7 and h5, hoping for a one-move mate. Defend correctly and the queen is just a target.

coach.text: 1.e4 e5 2.Qh5?! The queen attacks e5 and threatens Qxf7#. Do NOT panic. First defend f7 and develop: 2...Nc6 guards e5 and develops a piece. The threat of mate is met by simply covering f7 next.

The trap to AVOID: after 2...Nc6 3.Bc4, the natural-looking 3...Nf6?? walks into 4.Qxf7#. The bishop on c4 and queen on h5 both hit f7 — the king cannot take, every escape is covered. This is checkmate. Remember it so you never allow it.

The correct move is 3...g6! It hits the queen AND slams the door on f7. White's queen must scuttle back (4.Qf3 or 4.Qd1), and then 4...Nf6 develops with a tempo gained. Black is fine — the early queen has wasted moves while Black brought out pieces.

One more weakness to NEVER create against 1.e4: the move ...f6. This is the Damiano — 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 f6?? 3.Nxe5! If 3...fxe5 4.Qh5+ and the king is exposed; g6 5.Qxe5+ forks king and rook. ...f6 rips open the king's own diagonal. The lesson: defend f7 with PIECES (...Nc6, ...g6, ...Qe7), never with ...f6.

intro.text: White has played 2.Qh5 Nc6 3.Bc4, threatening Qxf7#. Find the one move that defends f7, hits the queen, and keeps everything sound.

coach.text: Black to move. Qxf7# is threatened (queen + bishop both hit f7). One pawn move solves everything.

successLine.text: 3...g6! Perfect. The pawn blocks the queen's path to f7 and attacks her at the same time. She retreats, you play ...Nf6 next, and White's premature queen raid has only lost him time.

retryLine.text: That doesn't stop the mate. Qxf7# is coming. You need a move that BOTH blocks the f7 path and gains time. A pawn to g6 does both — it covers f7 and attacks the queen.

intro.text: When the opponent's king or queen comes out too early, you punish it. Train kingside-attack and exposed-king tactics.

lessons.title: Play It: The Scandinavian in Action
lessons.summary: Warm-up tactics, then play the Scandinavian against the engine as Black.

intro.text: Last reps before the live game. Sharpest patterns from the open positions you've been building.

One last check of the core idea before you play — on a fresh move order. This time you reached it via 2...Qxd5 3.Nf3 c6 4.Nc3, so ...c6 is already in. The knight hits your queen; pick either main retreat.

coach.text: Black to move — the c3-knight attacks the queen, and you have already played ...c6. Retreat to one of the two active homes.

successLine.text: Good — a5 or d6 again, on a board you hadn't seen. With ...c6 already played, Nb5 can never harass her. Now you know the whole plan: ...Nf6, ...Bf5, ...e6, ...Be7, castle. Go play it against a real opponent.

retryLine.text: Pick an ACTIVE, safe square — the queen belongs on a5 or d6. Avoid checks (they lose time) and avoid the passive d8 for this drill.

segments.title: Boss: play the Scandinavian as Black

bossIntro.text: Now you play it. You are Black. Answer 1.e4 with 1...d5, recapture with the queen, retreat her safely to a5 or d6, then build the house: ...Nf6, ...c6, ...Bf5, ...e6, ...Be7, castle. Keep the queen safe, develop with purpose, and don't grab poisoned pawns. Show me you understand the WHY.

questions.prompt: What is the whole point of meeting 1.e4 with 1...d5 (the Scandinavian)?
options: To win White's e-pawn by force
To strike at e4 immediately and deny White a big pawn centre
To trade queens as fast as possible
To set up a kingside pawn storm
questions.explain: 1...d5 hits e4 the moment it appears. White almost always takes (2.exd5), trading his centre pawn — Black gets an open, equal fight instead of letting White build e4+d4.

questions.prompt: After 1.e4 d5 2.exd5 Qxd5 3.Nc3, the knight attacks your queen. Which retreat is BAD?
options: 3...Qa5 — safe and active on the rim
questions.explain: 3...Qe5+ is met by a developing block like Be2 or Nge2 — White gains ANOTHER tempo chasing the queen. a5, d6 and d8 are all fine; a5 and d6 are the active main lines.

questions.prompt: In the Scandinavian set-up, why is the move ...c6 so important?
options: It opens a file for the rook
It guards b5 (stopping Nb5) and gives the queen the c7 escape square
It threatens to win a pawn on d4
It prepares a quick kingside castle
questions.explain: ...c6 is pure queen-safety prophylaxis: it kills the Nb5 jump that would harass the queen, and hands her a bolt-hole on c7. It's the glue of the whole defence.

questions.prompt: Which is the RIGHT order for developing Black's light-squared bishop and the e-pawn?
options: Leave the bishop home and play ...e5 immediately
Trade the bishop on c3 as soon as possible
questions.explain: The bishop must come to f5 BEFORE ...e6, or ...e6 locks it inside the pawn chain forever. Getting that bishop active is the key to a good Scandinavian.

questions.prompt: 1.e4 d5. White plays 2.exd5. Recapture and start the Scandinavian.
questions.explain: 2...Qxd5. The queen comes out early — that's the deal in the Scandinavian. You'll retreat her safely after 3.Nc3.

questions.prompt: A different move order: 1.e4 d5 2.exd5 Qxd5 3.Nf3 Nf6 4.Nc3. The knight has just hit your queen on d5 (you and White have each developed a knight). Send her to one of the two active homes.
questions.explain: 3...Qa5 or 3...Qd6 — the same decision you trained, on a fresh board. Both are safe and active; a check like ...Qe5+ just hands White a developing block with tempo.

questions.prompt: Your queen is safe on a5; White has played 4.d4 and 5.Nf3. Play the prophylactic pawn move that guards b5.
questions.explain: 5...c6! Stops Nb5 from harassing the queen and opens the c7 escape. Now ...Bf5 and ...e6 finish the set-up.

questions.prompt: You've played ...c6 and White developed 6.Bc4. Deploy the light-squared bishop to its IDEAL Scandinavian post — the f5-square, eyeing White's kingside — out of the chain before ...e6.
questions.explain: 6...Bf5! The thematic square. The bishop must escape before ...e6 buries it; on f5 it's active and eyes White's kingside. (...Bg4 is also playable, but f5 is the standard Scandinavian deployment we trained.)

questions.prompt: White declines the pawn with 2.Nc3?! instead of taking. Punish it the simplest way.
questions.explain: 2...dxe4 removes White's only centre pawn for an easy, equal game; 2...d4 grabbing space is also good. White's 2.Nc3 gives Black no trouble.

questions.prompt: A different early-queen raid: 1.e4 e5 2.Bc4 d6 3.Qh5. The bishop on c4 and queen on h5 both bear down on f7. Play the move that shields f7 AND hits the queen, gaining a tempo.
questions.explain: 3...g6! blocks the c4-bishop-and-queen battery on f7 and attacks the queen, so she must retreat (Qe2/Qf3/Qd1). Same idea as the drilled Wayward Queen — defend f7 with a pawn that also wins time.

questions.prompt: 1.e4 d5 2.exd5 Qxd5 3.Nc3 Qa5 4.Bc4. Black grabbed the knight with 4...Qxc3. Was that a good move?
questions.explain: Blunder. After 5.dxc3 (or 5.bxc3) White simply recaptures and Black is down a whole queen for a knight. Never grab a defended piece with the queen — queen safety first.

questions.prompt: 1.e4 e5 2.Qh5 Nc6 3.Bc4. Black, forgetting the threat, developed with 3...Nf6. Was that a good move?
questions.explain: Disaster — 3...Nf6?? allowed 4.Qxf7#. The queen and bishop both hit f7, the king can't take and has no escape. Against 2.Qh5 you must defend f7 (with ...g6), not ignore it.

questions.prompt: 1.e4 d5 2.exd5 Qxd5 3.Nc3 Qd6 4.d4 Nf6 5.Nf3 c6. White played the careless 6.Nb5, attacking the queen and c7. Was White's move good?
questions.explain: Bad for White — because Black played ...c6, the b5-square is covered: 6...cxb5! just wins the knight. This is exactly why ...c6 goes in early. Black is now up a piece.

questions.prompt: 1.e4 d5 2.exd5 Qxd5 3.Nc3 Qa5 4.d4 Nf6 5.Nf3 Bf5. Black developed the bishop OUT before ...e6. Was that a good move?
questions.explain: Correct — this is the model plan. The bishop reaches its active post on f5 before ...e6 can shut it in. A textbook Scandinavian development.

questions.prompt: 1.e4 d5 2.e5. White pushed past instead of taking. Black answered 2...c5, hitting the base of the chain. Was that a good move?
questions.explain: Correct. 2.e5 just gives Black a favourable French-style position: the e5-pawn is a target, and 2...c5 with ...Nc6 and ...Bf5 to follow leaves Black at least equal.
